title: FizzBuzz and Classic Algorithms
subtitle: Solve famous programming challenges to sharpen your skills

sections.heading: FizzBuzz and is_prime
sections.content:
FizzBuzz is the most famous beginner programming challenge: for numbers 1-20, print 'Fizz' if divisible by 3, 'Buzz' if by 5, 'FizzBuzz' if both, or the number otherwise.

The prime check tests divisibility up to the square root of n — a simple but clever optimisation.

sections.heading: Binary Search
sections.content: Binary search finds an item in a sorted list very efficiently. Instead of checking every item, it repeatedly halves the search space — finding any item in a million-item list in at most 20 steps!

exercises.title: FizzBuzz
exercises.prompt: Write the FizzBuzz solution for numbers 1 to 15. For multiples of 3 print 'Fizz', multiples of 5 print 'Buzz', multiples of both print 'FizzBuzz', otherwise print the number.
hints: Then check for Fizz (3), then Buzz (5)

exercises.title: Find All Primes Up to 50
exercises.prompt: Write is_prime(n) that returns True if n is prime. Then use a list comprehension to create a list of all primes from 2 to 50. Print the list.
exercises.starter_code:
def is_prime(n):
    # Return False for n < 2
    # Check divisibility from 2 to sqrt(n)
    pass

primes = [n for n in range(2, 51) if is_prime(n)]
print(primes)
hints: If n % i == 0, it is not prime — return False
If the loop finishes without finding a divisor, return True

quiz.question: In FizzBuzz, why must you check for FizzBuzz (divisible by both 3 AND 5) BEFORE checking for Fizz or Buzz alone?
options: If you check 3 first, 15 would print Fizz instead of FizzBuzz
Python evaluates conditions in reverse order
The and operator is faster than or
quiz.explanation: If you check 'n % 3 == 0' first, the number 15 matches that condition and prints 'Fizz' — then execution moves on. You never get to check if it is also divisible by 5. Checking the most specific condition (both) first avoids this.

quiz.question: Why does is_prime check divisors only up to sqrt(n)?
options: It is faster to start from the middle
If n has a factor larger than sqrt(n), it must also have one smaller than sqrt(n)
quiz.explanation: If n = a * b and a > sqrt(n), then b must be < sqrt(n). So if n has any factors at all, at least one must be <= sqrt(n). This means checking up to sqrt(n) is sufficient to prove primality.

completion_message: Excellent! These classic algorithms appear in coding interviews everywhere. You are ready!
